coredata.dc:title: The influence of combination mode on the structure and properties of graphene quantum dot-porphyrin composites
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xue
Zhang, Bing
Zhang, Qiang
Tang, Yiwei
Liu, Xiuying
Li, Jianrong
coredata.dc:description: As a rising star in the family of fluorescent material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention because of their excellent properties such as high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. Herein, strongly fluorescent GQDs have been prepared by one-step solvothermal method. Then three kinds of GQDs-porphyrin composites were assembled by means of noncovalent interaction. It was found that the functional groups on the peripheries of the porphyrin were important on the combination mode of porphyrin and GQDs. The fluorescence of GQDs was quenched in the presence of the porphyrin. The suggested quenching mechanism is through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Tetrasodium-meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphine dodecahydrate (NaTSPP) was easy to react with GQDs and flat on the GQDs due to the strong hydrogen bond and π-π interaction. The photoelectronic activity of the GQDs-NaTSPP composite was higher than those of the other two GQDs-porphyrin composites. These results provided some hints for how to adjust the properties of the GQDs-porphyrin composites by modifying the combination mode between porphyrins and GQDs. These novel GQDs-porphyrin composites hold promise for various optical and luminescence-based applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Porphyrin
Binding mode
Interaction
Property

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31501564 ), a grant from the National Key Technologies R & D Program of China during the 12th Five-Year Plan Period (No. 2012BAD29B06 ), the Research Project from Science & Technology Department of Liaoning Province of China (N0. 2015103020 ), the Team Support Program for the Taishan Scholar of Blue Industry leading personnel of shandong Province of China ( LZBZ2015-19 ), Open Foundation of Food Safety Key Laboratory of Liaoning Province ( LNSAKF2013012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of GQDs. The inset shows size distributions of GQDs.

A. UV–vis absorption spectra of the (a) GO and (b) GQDs. B. The excitation-dependent PL behavior of GQDs, when the GQDs were excited at wavelengths from 410 to 480 nm (10 nm interval), the PL peak shifts from 491 nm to 535 nm and the PL intensity decreases remarkably. The inset shoes the photograph taken under UV light.

A. UV–vis spectra of 50 μL 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L NaTSPP aqueous solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 0; b: 100 μL; c: 150 μL; d: 200 μL; e: 250 μL; f: 300 μL; g: 350 μL, GQDs diluted 10-fold). B. UV–vis spectra of 400 μL 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L FeTMPyP aqueous solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 0; b: 50 μL; c: 100 μL; d: 200 μL; e: 250 μL; f: 300 μL; g: 350 μL, GQDs diluted 10-fold). C. UV–vis spectra of 200 μL 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L TMOHPP DMF solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 0; b: 50 μL; c: 100 μL; d: 150 μL; e: 200 μL; f: 250 μL; g: 300 μL, GQDs diluted 10-fold).
Normalized absorbance and fluorescence spectra of GQDs and porphyrin solution.

A. Fluorescent spectra of 50 μL 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L NaTSPP aqueous solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 200 μL; b: 300 μL; c: 400 μL; d: 500 μL; e: 600 μL; f: 700 μL, GQDs diluted 10-fold). B. Fluorescent spectra of 400 μL 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L FeTMPyP aqueous solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 50 μL; b: 100 μL; c: 150 μL; d: 200 μL; e: 250 μL; f: 300 μL; g: 350 μL, GQDs diluted 10-fold). C. Fluorescent spectra of 200 μL 1.0 × 10-4 mol/L TMOHPP DMF solution after adding GQDs DMF solution with various volume (a: 50 μL; b: 100 μL; c: 150 μL; d: 200 μL; e: 250 μL; f: 300 μL; g: 400 μL).
Fluorescence spectra of GQDs solution after introducing porphyrin. NaTSPP(A), FeTMPyP(B), TMOHPP(C).

head.ce:title: The influence of combination mode on the structure and properties of graphene quantum dot-porphyrin composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University/National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products/Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province/The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
Bohai University/National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage

Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products/Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province/The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of College Foundation Education, Bohai University, Jinzhou, Liaoning, 121013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a rising star in the family of fluorescent material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention because of their excellent properties such as high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. Herein, strongly fluorescent GQDs have been prepared by one-step solvothermal method. Then three kinds of GQDs-porphyrin composites were assembled by means of noncovalent interaction. It was found that the functional groups on the peripheries of the porphyrin were important on the combination mode of porphyrin and GQDs. The fluorescence of GQDs was quenched in the presence of the porphyrin. The suggested quenching mechanism is through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Tetrasodium-meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl) porphine dodecahydrate (NaTSPP) was easy to react with GQDs and flat on the GQDs due to the strong hydrogen bond and π-π interaction. The photoelectronic activity of the GQDs-NaTSPP composite was higher than those of the other two GQDs-porphyrin composites. These results provided some hints for how to adjust the properties of the GQDs-porphyrin composites by modifying the combination mode between porphyrins and GQDs. These novel GQDs-porphyrin composites hold promise for various optical and luminescence-based applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has attracted tremendous attention because of its extraordinary mechanical, thermal, chemical and optical properties as well as potential applications [1,2]. Graphene is a thin film like hexagonal honeycomb lattice which composed of carbon atoms by sp2 hybrid orbitals [3]. The size of the graphene materials is mostly micron level, which limits their application in nano device. So it can be changed to nanoscale, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs as the new member of the family of graphene are a new type of QDs. GQDs are luminescent emerging kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [4–9]. Compere to graphene, GQDs not only have special physical and chemical properties of graphene, but also have strong fluorescence, high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. GQDs have been fabricated by electro-beam lithography [10] or ruthenium-catalyzed C60 transformation [11]. These methods are limited by the requirement for special equipment, extremely expensive raw materials and low yield. Herein, we report a new method for the synthesis of strongly green-photoluminescent GQDs in large scale. A one-step solvothermal route from graphene oxide is implemented. Due to the stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility and biocompatibility, the GQDs are particularly eco-friendly and are demonstrated to be excellent probes for bioimaging.

Porphyrins are planar molecules with a large macrocycle which binds cyclic compounds coaxially [12]. This type of binding has been shown to occur even when the compound bears nitrogen [13] or a metal has been coordinated to the central nitrogen atoms of porphyrin [14]. Porphyrins strongly absorb visible light, with the highest extinction coefficients found in the blue region of the spectrum. This feature makes porphyrins be useful for functional nanostructures applied in solar energy conversion [15], photodynamic therapy [16], nanoelectronic circuits [17] and photocatalysis [18]. Porphyrins are a type of macrocyclic organic molecule which have an extensive system of delocalized π electrons. The binding and catalytic characteristics of porphyrins can be altered through varying the peripheral substituent groups or through metal incorporation via coordination to the central nitrogen atoms. Porphyrins are an important class of naturally macrocyclic compounds with large conjugated π-electron system that can be used in the assemblies of donor-acceptor materials, in molecular electronics and photovoltaic devices [19].

Nanocomposites of porphyrins with carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerenes (C60), graphene and graphene oxide have been widely explored in the literature with interesting applications in many areas [20–24]. However, not much is known about the characterization and photophysical behaviors of GQDs and porphyrins nanocomposites. Recently, the fluorescence behavior of GQDs in the presence of porphyrins was reported [25,26]. Another study reported the application of GQDs assembled with porphyrins as a probe for analytes sensing [27]. Fe (II) porphyrin loaded onto GQDs surface has been employed as a photocatalyst for the oxidation of alcohols [28]. GQDs-porphyrin nanocomposites and their potentials are still relatively unexplored. Hence, it is highly imperative that more efforts be channeled in this direction.

This work reports for the first time on the fluorescence and absorption properties of GQDs-porphyrin nanoconjugates. Three kinds of porphyrins with different functional groups are chosen to modify GQDs through noncovalent interaction in order to deeply understand the relationships for the GQDs-porphyrin composites. Due to the difference of functional groups on the peripheries of porphyrin, the interaction and binding mode between the GQDs and the porphyrins are different, which lead to various performances for the GQDs-porphyrin composites. It is expected that the fluorescence of GQDs will be quenched in the presence porphyrin as is the case with semiconductors QDs. Fluorescence quenching of GQDs by porphyrin is well documented as well as FRET as the interaction mechanism between GQDs and porphyrin.

Graphite powder with the metals basis of 99.95% (325 mech), Phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5, ≥98.0%) and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Potassium peroxydisulfate (K2S2O8) were purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and Hydrogen peroxide (30%) were purchased from Tianli Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) was purchased from Jinzhou Chemical Reagent Company. Tetrasodium-meso-tetra (4-sulfonatophenyl) porphine dodecahydrate (NaTSPP, 95%), Fe(III) meso-Tetra(N-Methyl- 4-Pyridyl) porphine pentachloride (FeTPyP) and meso-Tetra(3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenyl) porphine (TMOHPP) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. The molecular structures of the three porphyrins were supplied in Scheme S1(see the Supplementary Materials). The water used in all experiments had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ/cm. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Fluoromax-4NIR spectra fluorophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, NJ, USA), and 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette was used in experiments. All pH measurements were made with a PHS-3C pH meter (Tuopu Co., Hangzhou, China).

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared with a modified Hummers method [29]. (1) Preoxidation: 1.0 g graphite powder was mixed with H2SO4 (12 mL), K2S2O8 (2.5 g) and P2O5 (2.5 g) carefully, the mixture was heated for 4.5 h in 80 ℃. Then add 0.5 L deionized water, filtering and washing to remove excess acid. The product dried under vacuum in 45 ℃ for 10 h. (2) The oxidation process: This process was composed of three steps as low temperature, medium temperature, high temperature. First of all, concentrated H2SO4 (30 mL) was added to a mixture of graphite (1.0 g), and the mixture was cooled using an ice bath to 6 °C. KMnO4 (4.0 g) was added slowly in portions to keep the reaction temperature below 12 °C. The reaction was warmed to 35 °C and stirred for 7 h. Medium temperature react was removed to the ice water bath, and the reaction was stirred for 2 h at 35 °C. High temperature react was stirred for 90 min at 90 ℃, and adding 50 mL deionized water, make it boiling for 25 min. Finally, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and poured onto deionized water (125 mL) with 30% H2O2 (10 mL), and the mixture was aged for 24 h. The mixture was filtered and washed with 1:10 HCl solution (1000 mL) in order to remove metal ions, then washed with a large amount of distilled water. The GO product was suspended in distilled water to give a brown, 2% dispersion, which was subjected to dialysis to completely remove metal ions and acids (7 days, changed deionized water every 12 h). The solid GO were obtained by vacuum drying at 45 °C.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was prepared with the GO through one-step solvothermal method [30,31]. The first 810 mg GO powder scattered over 60 ml DMF ultrasound (120 W, 53 kHz) dispersed for 90 min. Then obtained solutions were transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200℃ for 8 h. After this reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally and filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. The brown filtrate was GQDs dispersion in the DMF.

The GQDs-NaTSPP composite was prepared by adding GQDs DMF dispersion into NaTSPP aqueous solution. 2.5 × 10−6 mol/L NaTSPP aqueous solution was mixed with a certain amount of GQDs DMF dispersion and shaking for 10 min. Then the GQDs-NaTSPP composite was obtained. In the same process, by replacing NaTSPP with FeTMPyP and TMOHPP, the GQDs-FeTMPyP composite and GQDs-TMOHPP composite were obtained.

FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Scimitar 2000 Near FT-IR Spectrometer (USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Fluoromax-4NIR fluorescence spectrophotometer (France). Raman spectra were measured on a LabRAM HR Evdution Raman microscope with a 532 nm DPSS laser and a 50×objective (NA = 0.42) (France). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken with an S-4800(Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken with a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope device (USA), and the accelerating voltage was 200 kV.

GQDs have been prepared via one-step solvothermal method from graphene oxide. Fig. 1 showed TEM images of GQDs and their size distributions. The GQDs were monodispersed with an overall quasi spherical morphology with particles size ranging from 0.88 to 3.24 nm, with an average diameter of 1.82 nm.

Fig. 2 A-a was the UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersion of GO. We can see a main absorption peak at 230 nm and a shoulder peak at 310 nm, which were due to π-π* transition of CC bonds and n-π* transition of CO bonds, respectively. And its adsorption peaks were similar to the GO samples which reported in literature [32]. Fig. 2A-b was the UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs. Comparing with Fig. 2A-a, there was no absorption peak at 230 nm and we can see a typical absorption peak at about 310 nm similar to the reported GQDs [22,33]. Inset of Fig. 2 was the photograph of GQDs in the DMF solution taken under visible and UV lights, the green fluorescent color was observed by the naked eye. All GQDs showed the excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. When the excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 480 nm, the PL peak shifted from 491 nm to 535 nm and the PL intensity was decreased obviously (Fig. 2B). This excitation-dependent PL behavior in fluorescent carbon nanomaterial was extensive, it was because of the optical choose of differently sized GQDs and surface defects of GQDs [34–38]. So far, the GQDs showed the high light stability.

In order to improve the understanding of the types of interaction between the GQDs and the porphyrins, we conducted an investigation by the UV–vis spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there were the UV–vis spectrums of the NaTSPP before and after adding the GQDs solution. In the absence of GQDs, NaTSPP showed a Soret band at 412 nm probably because of a1u (π) to eg* (π) transition. In addition, four weak absorption peaks at 515, 549, 578 and 631 nm in the Q bands were due to the a2u (π) to eg* (π) transition. The spectral characteristics were consistent with the previously reported [39,40]. With increase of the GQDs concentration, the position, width and intensity of the absorption spectra of NaTSPP were changed. The most obvious change occurred in the Soret band region of the absorption spectrum: the intensity of the original Soret band at 412 nm decreased gradually, a new Soret band appeared at 433 nm and its intensity increased. With different results reported in Ref. [37], the number and position of Q bands changed. Three of the four Q bands disappeared completely, at about 645 nm produced a new broad band. This meant that the introduction of the GQDs made the NaTSPP plane distort [41]. It can be explained that, in this complex, oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxy(−OH), carboxyl(−COOH) and epoxy (COC) on the edge and basal plane made GQDs easily react with NaTSPP through the hydrogen bond and π-π interaction. Such NaTSPP molecular sat flat on the surface of the GQDs like porphyrin J-aggregation [42]. It influenced the electron distribution as well as electron/energy transfer between NaTSPP and GQDs, which was reflected by the red-shifted Soret band and the three disappeared Q bands.

As shown in Fig. 3B, the UV–vis spectrum of FeTMPyP at 422 nm had a very high intense Soret band, while 595 nm and 636 nm weak Q band was due to the vibration of porphyrin electronic [43]. The typical metalloporphyrin had a small amount of Q band. When the metal ion coordinated with the N atoms, the symmetry of the molecule increased so the number of Q bands reduced [44]. With the increase of GQDs concentration, the intensity of the absorption spectrum of the GQDs-FeTPyP solution, width and position were changed. The intensity of the Q band and the end of the absorption band were gradually reduced, the intensity of Soret band at 422 nm gradually weakened and widen. The blue shift of 4 nm at Soret band proved the interaction between GQDs and FeTPyP. The results showed that the delocalized π bonds decreased the average electron density of FeTPyP which increased the energy available for electron transition as a result, a blue shift of the Soret bands occurred. FeTPyP was positively charged because of the presence of pyridyl groups, so in the presence of FeTPyP, GQDs easily interacted with FeTPyP by electrostatic interaction and π-π interaction stacking [45].

As shown in Fig. 6C, In the absence of GQDs, the Soret absorption peak of TMOHPP was at 424 nm, the four weak Q bands absorption peak were at 521, 560, 595 and 652 nm. When adding GQDs, the Soret absorption peak of TMOHPP at 424 nm disappeared and a new absorption peak appeared near 433 nm. With the increment of GQDs, the new absorption peak occurred bathochromic shift and its strength increased. And the four Q bands disappeared gradually, at about 688 nm produced a new broad band absorption peak and its strength increased, at the same time it occurred bathochromic shift also. These results indicated that the functional groups on the surface and edge of GQDs played an important role in the π-π interactions between TMOHPP and GQDs. The noncovalent action of GQDs with various molecules through supermolecular interactions such as π-π stacking, electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding were reported [46]. Since TMOHPP existed hydroxide radical, it was expected that interactions between TMOHPP and GQDs might have been through hydrogen bonding interaction and π-π stacking.

It was reported that, when the transition of electrons was from the center of the porphyrin macrocycle to the edge, the absorption will red-shift; otherwise, blue-shift [40,41,47]. So, the red-shift in the UV spectra of the GQDs-NaTSPP and the GQDs-TMOHPP ascribed to the transition of electron atmosphere from the center of the porphyrin macrocycle to the edge. It may be attributed to the stronger interaction between GQDs and NaTSPP. Such spectroscopic evidence suggested that the GQDs was selective toward NaTSPP, TMOHPP and FeTMPyP.

In order to discuss three kinds of the interactions of GQDs-porphyrin composites, the fluorescence spectra of GQDs-NaTSPP, GQDs-FeTMPyP and GQDs-TMOHPP composites were measured (Figs. 4 and 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6, it was found that the fluorescence of GQDs was greatly quenched after introducing porphyrin. As reported [41,47], the fluorescence quenching could be attributed to FRET, or non-radiative dipole-dipole coping between the fluorescence species and GQDs [48]. This quenching efficiency of the GQDs-porphyrin composite mainly due to the efficient electron/energy transfer on the interface of GQDs and porphyrin.

It was reported that hydrogen bond was as efficient as π- or δ-bonding to energy transfer [49]. The functional groups on the edge of the porphyrins made a different way in linking mode and interaction with the GQDs. The results showed that the energy/electron transfer between GQDs and porphyrins was dependent on the interaction magnitude.

To demonstrate the difference of binding strength between GQDs and the three porphyrins, the apparent binding constant (Kα) between GQDs and porphyrin is calculated according to the equation listed below [50]:
(1) F0/F = 1+ Kα[Q]
where F0 and F are the maximum value of the fluorescence intensity for GQDs in the absence and presence of porphyrin, respectively, and [Q] is the concentration of porphyrin. The apparent binding constant (Kα) is Kα(GQDs-NaTSPP) = 1.014 × 106 M−1, Kα(GQDs-FeTMPyP) = 6.268 × 104 M-1 and Kα(GQDs-TMOHPP) = 1.553 × 105 M−1, respectively. It is indicated that the binding strength between GQDs and the porphyrin is in order of GQDs-NaTSPP>GQDs-TMOHPP>GQDs-FeTMPyP.

Combining the results in Figs. 3–6 with the reported principles [51,52], we found three different binging mechanisms ruled the interaction between the GQDs and the porphyrins (NaTSPP, FeTMPyP and TMOHPP). Based on the structural characteristics of the TMOHPP and the GQDs, it was deduced that the interaction between the TMOHPP and the GQDs mainly occurred on the peripheries of the GQDs. In the case of NaTSPP, the sulfo substituents and the NaTSPP core had a cooperative effect, which played the important role in the location of NaTSPP molecules, and the NaTSPP molecules flatted on the surface of GQDs just like porphyrin J-aggregation. While the possible interaction between FeTMPyP plane and GQDs sheet was electrostatic interaction and π-π interaction stacking. Based on the above analysis, it was deduced that three porphyrins and GQDs may adopt the binding modes as Scheme S2 (see the Supplementary Materials), respectively.

In this study, we synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and studied their fluorescence and absorption behavior in the presence of porphyrins. Noncovalent interaction (π-π, hydrogen bond, electrostatic) of GQDs and porphyrins was shown in absorption and fluorescence spectrum studies. The fluorescence emission of GQDs was found to be efficiently quenched by the porphyrins and the fluorescence emission of porphyrins efficiently enhanced by the GQDs due to Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET). This study provides fundamental insights into host-guest nanoensembles of GQDs and porphyrins which are essential prerequisites to harnessing the synergistic properties of GQDs and porphyrins for applications in nanosensors fabrication, photo- catalysis, bio-imaging and drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31501564), a grant from the National Key Technologies R & D Program of China during the 12th Five-Year Plan Period (No. 2012BAD29B06), the Research Project from Science & Technology Department of Liaoning Province of China (N0. 2015103020), the Team Support Program for the Taishan Scholar of Blue Industry leading personnel of shandong Province of China (LZBZ2015-19), Open Foundation of Food Safety Key Laboratory of Liaoning Province (LNSAKF2013012).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.08.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. Bartelme, D.M. Guldi, John Wiley and Sons, Inc., Hoboken, NJ, USA. 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Photochem. Photobiol. C Photochem. Rev.
